speaker: interviewer
text: October 2, 1995, Rita Childs, maiden name Schachter. Interviewer, Andrea Gootnick. City, State, Thousand Oaks, California. Country, USA. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name's Andrea Gootnick. I am interviewing Rita Childs. It is October 2, 1995. We are in Thousand Oaks, California in the United States of America. Can you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name now is Rita Childs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-I-T-A C-H-I-L-D-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your maiden name, your birth name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Erika, E-R-I-K-A, Schachter. C-H-- S-C-H-A-C-H-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on May 19, 1921, in Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your father's name? What was your father's--?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Joseph Schachter. And he owned a-- he was a furrier. He was a-- what they call a furrier. He had a-- a business, not in Vienna. When I was a year old, my parents, because of the anti-Semitism in Vienna, moved to Germany. We-- I was brought up in Dusseldorf, which is on the River Rhine next to Cologne. Everybody knows Cologne. And I've lived there-- my early childhood was lived in Dusseldorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father had a business, a store in one of the nicest section of the city, comparable to Fifth Avenue in New York or-- or-- what is the one in-- in Beverly Hills? Rodeo Drive. He had a nice store. And then he opened up a couple of more stores in neighboring towns. We did-- he did very well in-- in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to a-- a private school. When I was six, I started school. By that time, I already knew a little bit. I knew how to write my name, and-- and I knew the alphabet and I could read a little and went to a private school. And of course, in Germany at that time, religion was part of the curriculum. So we were excused for that. Usually they did it early in the morning, so we came an hour later. But we had to go to a religious school. I went to a Hebrew school twice a week. I don't remember much about it. We tried to learn Hebrew and the holidays and all of this. But we used to bribe the rabbi with cookies and stuff so he wouldn't be too hard on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back. What is your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Lena, and her maiden name was Richter, R-I-C-H-T-E-R. And she was born in Vienna and-- and so was my grandmother, who lived with us as long as I remember. She was a widow, and in those days, widows-- women alone did not live alone. They lived with their children, [? one or-- ?] usually their daughters. So every woman, even though she wanted a son, she hoped to have a daughter so she could live with the daughter. It's usually the daughter that she lived with. And my mother-- my grandmother-- her name was Rosa Richter-- she lived with us as long as I can remember in our place. She always had her own room. And she did a lot of the cooking. She was a fantastic cook. She was-- she called herself Hungarian. I think she was Viennese. But when she was born, it was the Austrian-Hungary Empire. And Austria, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, part of-- of a lot of these countries were all in one empire. It was called the Habsburg Empire.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she spoke-- she and my mother used to speak Hungarian if they didn't want us to know what they were talking about. I never learned the language except for a few words. She used to say [HUNGARIAN], which means oh, my god.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandmother lived with us as long, as I said, as long as I can remember. And she did not particularly like to live with this daughter because she had another daughter that she favored. Her name was Charlotte [NON-ENGLISH]. But she was-- she married a non-Jewish man, and there was no way she could have lived with her. And she died very young. I think she was in her 30s when she died. I don't know what she died of. But my cousin Elsa-- that was her-- her daughter-- came to live with us when she was a teenager because her father married somebody not much older than his daughter, and they didn't get along at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain who Elsa-- Elsa--

speaker: interviewee
text: Elsa is [NON-ENGLISH] child, my grandmother's daughter, the other daughter, my mother's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So [NON-ENGLISH] was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember her. I used to see picture, but I don't remember her at all in my memory. She died when she was in her 30s, and I must have been a child. So I don't remember. I remember my cousin Elsa. I have some pictures of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have any other brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she had-- she had a brother in Vienna, Artur. He was a dentist. And he lived in a very nice section of Vienna, downtown-- what they would call here downtown, right across the street from the-- from the-- from the Rathaus. You know what a rathaus is? It's like the city hall. But it was a beautiful building, you know? It was beautiful down there and the park and everything. And he had his office and his home-- he had his office in his house, in his home. He had a big apartment, and two rooms were his dentistry and the others were-- and she had another brother who lived in Czechoslovakia. I don't remember his name, except his name was Mannie. They called him Mannie. I don't remember his last name. Well, his last name was Richter, you know. They were all Richters. And I once visited them in the summer. I will get to that later. And they lived in a little town. I don't even know the name of it. But it was like a ghetto. They were all very religious Jews. My parents were not religious. My father was brought up in a town that was Austria when he was born. And then it became-- after the First World War, when everything was cut up, it became Romania. He was born in [? Czernowitz, ?] Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Czernowitz. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Czernowitz ?] was the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how that is spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-E-R-N-O-W-I-T-Z, I think. I'm not-- I think that's about it. And I never remember having seen his parents, but I must have when I was a child. We must have gone there, but I don't remember it. But they didn't come to Vienna. They didn't come to visit. Nobody came to Germany. I met all my-- my relatives when we went back to Vienna, which was a big mistake, of course. But I never-- I only heard about them. When we were living in Germany, we were pretty much on our own. And-- and the story about my parents is really interesting, too. Usually, in those days, the girls-- the-- the parents found husbands for them, but not in my-- in my parents' case. My father was in the First World War, and he was wounded. And my mother had a friend who had a husband who was wounded. And she went to the hospital with him to visit him, and my father was in the next bed. And that's how they met, and they fell in love. And when he came out of the hospital, he went to her parents to ask permission to write to her. That was very, very-- that he had to ask for permission, or he couldn't have written to her. And she wrote to-- to him. And then when the war was over, a year after the war, they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when, approximately, when they were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They married-- I was born in '21. I think they married either in 1918, 1919 or 1920. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were they approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- here. My father was born-- ah, what happened to my glasses? Can I get my glasses over there? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can't read without my glasses. Thank you. OK. My father was born in 19-- 1891 on March the 3rd. So he was-- if they got married in 1920, he was about-- let me see, nine and 20. He was 29. My mother was born in 19-- 1895. She was born on July 4. And she was a small woman. My father was tall. Unfortunately, I took after my mother. I was never very tall, to my chagrin. And so they had a very, very good marriage. They were always in love. I mean, they never did anything without each other. I mean, my father didn't play golf or went with clubs or anything like this. Everything he did was with the family. And my mother saved a lot of the letters that he wrote to her, and they were really wonderful. She had a whole box of them. And they always started, love of my life, my dearest heart, or something like that. They were very romantic letters. And I remember her showing them to me. They were very, very close. I mean, there was no question about the fact that they were in love.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your father's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't, because I don't remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only one I ever met that came to us was a-- a cousin. And I don't know whether it was from his brother or sister. I don't even know whether he had brothers or sisters. I have no idea. Yeah, he had a sister that lived in Vienna. But the others, I don't know. They never came to visit us. And we must have visited [INAUDIBLE] was a child. But I don't remember that. I'm quite sure we went there, but I don't remember anything of the sort. Some people remember when they were three years old. I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the-- you mentioned the religion in your family, the Judaism.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So both my father and my mother were brought up in religious homes-- Orthodox homes. And when they got married, they both decided that they didn't want any part of that. It was too cumbersome. It wouldn't let you live. It-- they told you what to eat, what-- they didn't want it. So they decided we're going to be Jewish, but not Orthodox. And when my grandmother Rosa came to live with us, she had to deal with that, because she was religious. But she had no choice. My mother said, I'm not going to run my house according to your rules. This is my house. And we're not [? calling it ?] a kosher house. And she-- she lived with that. I mean, she was not too happy. She wasn't a happy woman anyway, because she was-- she was widowed a long time. She lived with us as long as I can remember. She was widowed a long time. And my grandfather must have been fairly young when he died. And she was an unhappy woman. Not only this, we lived in Germany, and her family was in Vienna. And in those days, people just didn't come back and forth or anything like that. I don't ever remember her saying, I'm going to go visit my sisters or brothers in Vienna. She never did. She was always with us. Whatever we did, she was part of it. And she did a lot of the cooking because my mother went to the store with my father. She worked in the store. So she did a lot of the cooking. She was a wonderful cook. I wish I would have her danishes that she made and her strudels. She was a wonderful baker and cooker. And she learned to cook without being kosher. I mean, she grumbled, and she was an unhappy woman. And she and my father always had words. But it wasn't very serious because she knew there was nothing she could do. She had no place to go, because her sisters and her brothers wouldn't have taken her. They could have probably taken her in. She would have been more happy there. But, in those days, widows lived with their daughters. That was the rule. And the rule-- that kind of a rule was hard to break, I guess. Because I couldn't understand, when we went to Vienna she had all those relatives, and not one of them ever said, you can come and live with us. They had room. But-- especially she had a brother who never married who was also a dentist. And he lived alone with-- with the housekeeper. She certainly could have lived with him. But he never said so, and she never said so. She always lived with us until she died. This was the-- I guess this was the norm in those days, as far as I know. Because I don't know any-- all the other widows I knew, they always lived with their daughters. I don't know what they did if they didn't have daughters. I guess they had to live with their daughter-in-laws, which wasn't too happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your father's business.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a-- a-- a store where he made the coats and jackets out of fur, and to order, and some of them were not to order. And they could-- you could go in there and  buy a fur coat or had one made to order. He had a large sort of like a sewing room in the back of the store. The store was beautiful, as I said. It was in the nicest part, most like Rodeo Drive or Saks Fifth Avenue, Saks Fifth Avenue. It was in the nicest part of the town, and that was his main store. And then he-- then he had a couple of other stores. One was in [? Essen, ?] which is a little town right around-- not too far from there. And [NON-ENGLISH] is another town around there. I think he had three stores. There may have been another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did very well. We never had a house. But we lived in a very large apartment and we had a live-in maid. And while we were young, we had what they call a-- a French-- what do you call that? Somebody who took care of the children. Forgot the name of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: If she-- if she could be, she was French. And if not, she was German. But mostly, she was French, a mademoiselle, that took just care of the children. And they all lived with us. They each had their own room. And as I said, my-- my grandmother did most of the cooking. So the-- the one that took care of the house just did the cleaning [INAUDIBLE]. And we had a beautiful apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had-- we used to go on trips down the Rhine on a boat, which I remember to this day. They were wonderful. We stopped at where-- you heard of the Lorelei? There was this big rock where the Lorelei was supposed to have sat there. And there are old castles. They're still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to go there one day. But I got there in September, and it only runs in the-- in-- in the-- in the summer. And we used to go down the Rhine and stop at some of these old towns, had picnics and all this. We had a wonderful life at that time. My father was well-to-do. At one point, he even bought a car. But he didn't drive, so we had a chauffeur that drove us around. Especially on Sundays, we would go someplace. And you know, birthdays were celebrated very big. I had a lot of people coming to my-- sometimes a whole bunch of girls and me would be taken someplace, to a nice place with rides and stuff, what you would call a park, you know, here. And I remember wonderful things. And just before, in the 1931 or '32-- oh yes, in 1931, I finished school, you know, elementary school. And I-- my father enrolled me in what they call a lyceum, which is a girl's school, instead of a gymnasium. Gymnasiums were only for boys. They were strictly separated. And lyceums were for boys, but this was a higher school, a very, very good school. In order to get in, you would have to have a day-long test. Because out of-- they may have taken in 30 to 60 girls, and usually hundreds would apply. So I was scared. And I was told if I make it into the school, I would get my own wristwatch, my first wristwatch. I was 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was in '31, right, [? it was. ?] And so we-- I had to go to the school. I remember that day correctly. We all sat in a great big room. Here it would be a gymnasium. There it's called an [NON-ENGLISH], where all the-- the people that were-- you know, had applied came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the director got up, and he made a big speech and said that this was a school only for people that really wanted to learn. And that if you finished this school, you could go into any university that you wanted to. Any university would accept somebody that graduated from this school. So this was really something you had to really work at.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got scared to death. But then he read off some names and saying that people-- there were people that had such good grades and were so highly recommended they didn't have to take the test, and I was one of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never forgot that. I was like jubilant, because I was-- you know, everybody-- tests in Europe, where really people used to be sick about them. You know, later on, I don't whether you read about it, people committed suicide because of these tests. They were really very, very tough. And I got my wristwatch. And that summer we went to Italy for a month. We-- we stayed at the Isle of Capri. And I have a picture there where I swam in the-- in the Mediterranean waters. And it was a wonderful summer. It was wonderful. And when we came back, I started in the school. And I didn't think it was all that difficult. One of the things we took was French. And I did learn-- in the year and a half or two years that I was there, I learned French because you had a lot of homework. And there was no fudging or-- you had to do your work. And-- and the most important thing was all of our-- all of our teachers were men, and they were all professors-- PhDs. And they would-- they wouldn't take any, you know, fuss or anything. You couldn't-- you had to do your work, and that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did fairly well, except in-- in math. I wasn't so good in math. So I got a tutor for math. But I did well in French. I did well in German. German is a difficult language. I don't know whether you know, but the-- the grammar in German alone can kill you. You learn this all your life. You know, right? Isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When people tell me here today-- when I came here, said, oh, English is the hardest language, I said, English is [? not a ?] language. To me, that was nothing to learn English. It was the easiest language I've ever heard. Had to know grammar. The only thing difficult in the English language is the spelling. But German and French grammar is very difficult. And as I said, I did a lot of other things. I learned to swim when I was five, and I was a very good swimmer-- the breaststroke. I never learned to crawl because I didn't-- the-- apparently, the-- the chlorine in the water bothered my sinuses, so I gave it up. And I couldn't-- I-- they tried to teach me how to dive. And that also bothered me, so the teacher said forget about it. Just swim. I got sick from it. So to this day, I only swim the breaststroke. But I swim every day. [? We have ?] a pool right here. I swim every day, if I can. In the winter-- this is not heated, so in the winter I can't swim here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other hobbies did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I rode a bicycle. I remember, at one point, I got a bicycle, and I was allowed to drive to school on a bicycle, which was really something because it was-- in those days, not many people did that. Not many girls did that. Boys did it, but not girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was good in gymnasium. I remember doing a lot of gym things on the-- what do you call it? On the rings, you know? I remember I did that. And we had some ballgames.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was always called the last one because I was the shortest one always in the-- when we lined up, we had to line up according to height. And I was always at the end-- not the very end, but usually at the, you know, towards the end because I was always short. And that was my bane, but that was all. And I was very skinny at that point. It's hard to remember. My mother was-- because I didn't like milk. And I found out later why. I was allergic to it. So I never liked it, and I didn't want to drink milk, and I didn't want to eat cereal. And so I was a hard-- hard thing to bring up, I guess. Then my-- my brother was born in Dusseldorf in-- in 1925. And I remember very vaguely that my mother said she had to have a boy. She had to have a boy. Every Jewish family had to have a boy. That was very important to her. I found out later-- she told me this at one time-- that she shouldn't have had a second child because she wasn't well. But she had the child, even though it might have really hurt her very seriously, because she had to have a boy. And she did have a boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what is your brother's name? What was your brother's name. His-- him was Alfons. But we called him-- as long as I remember we called him [? Booby, ?] like little boy, you know? He was always [? Booby. ?] Nobody ever called him Alfons. And his middle name was [? Adolph, ?] I remember, believe it or not, which he dropped, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, he-- when we grew up, we were not-- we were almost four years apart, and to me, he was a pest. You know, we had the usual little fights that we had, until he went to school. Then we had a little better relationship. And my other-- my other hobby was reading. I was a voracious reader. I could read-- I used to take-- sometimes have a candle, until my mother found out and she-- you know, in bed and read late at night. Because, first of all, my parents had a wonderful library. They had lots and lots of books. And I was free to go to the library. That was very close to us always. And I used to take tons of books. I mean, I never took one or two books. I always took tons of books. And I was reading and reading and reading. I remember at one point, my father once came to me. I was sitting at a table, I was reading. And he hit his hand on the book and he says, for 10 minutes I've been trying to get your attention. And he couldn't get my attention because I was so involved in the book. I still remember what I was reading-- the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of books did you like to read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. As long as it was in print, I read it. I read books that were way beyond my age. I mean, I've read German literature. I read French literature. And I read Russian literature and English literature. I mean, I went to a Shakespeare play when I was six or seven-- it was translated into German-- and I loved it. And I read French literature. I loved French literature. And I also read the Russian literature.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, all the plays, Ibsen's plays. I mean, I was talking to somebody else-- did you know this? I said yes. And we also went to-- before I was 10 I went to the opera. My first opera was And we went to concerts.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, there was-- children were taken always. They-- they were-- and they were well-behaved. Nobody went up and down and said, I want this and that. They-- they sat. And when I was there, you know, in the '70s, I went someplace in a-- to a-- to an opera, and there was a man with two little children. They were little. They sat perfectly quiet. They never said, I'm tired, or I want something to drink or-- they sat perfectly quiet, well-behaved, and-- and listened. To this day, that's what they do. The children were brought up so when they said, you don't talk, you don't talk. I mean-- But on the other hand, it was stifling in a way that I didn't learn very much. I remember when I got my period, I was 11. It was very early. And I didn't know what I had. All I knew was-- was-- I was bloody. And I was afraid to tell my mother because I had gone swimming the day before, and I thought maybe I hurt myself, and I didn't want her to know. But she found out, and then she explained to me. The only thing she said, you're going to have this from now on every month. But she didn't explain to me what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So two years later-- it was two years later that somebody said, you know, you can find these things out in the library. So I went, you know, with some girls, and I got some books. And that's how I found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother never told me anything. That's very strange. Because in those days, children were supposed to be-- not to know too much so they wouldn't get worried, or they wouldn't-- it would hurt them. I don't know why, but that's the way it was. And I also learned to play the piano. We had a beautiful Bechstein grand piano in a special room that just had the piano where my parents entertained. And I remember, vaguely remember, that one of my brothers-- my father's sister-- got married in our apartment, because there were a lot of people there. I vaguely-- I must have been very little. And he was like-- because maybe his father had died, and he was the head of the house or something. I don't know why. But that's-- I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your apartment a [? bit. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very large. We each had our own room. And the-- the mademoiselle had their own room, and the maid had a room, and my parents had a bedroom. And then we had a-- a large living/sitting-- a dining/sitting room. In those days in-- in Europe, the living room had a huge table. So if you had guests, you automatically set them around the table because you had to serve coffee and cake. There was no such thing as just coming in for, you know-- [INAUDIBLE] somebody came, they were served coffee and cake on the best dishes-- homemade cake usually. And that was the-- the norm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was a huge, huge room. And we had these old credenzas with my mother's [INAUDIBLE] glasses and stuff. She had beautiful things. And [INAUDIBLE] our apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember too-- oh, we had a bathroom, which-- you know, with a heater. When you wanted to take-- you had to turn on the heater and-- to have hot water, because there was no running hot water in those days, even though Dusseldorf was an industrial city and much more advanced than some of the other cities were. It was a very modern type of city. [? So you want-- ?]
